skill.name: openclaw-medical-skills
skill.description: Codex adaptation of the OpenClaw Medical Skills library. Use for biomedical, clinical, healthcare AI, genomics, bioinformatics, drug discovery, pharmacovigilance, clinical trials, medical imaging, public health, medical device, regulatory, scientific data analysis, lab automation, and medical research workflows; also use when the user mentions OpenClaw Medical Skills, medical skill library, or any named capability preserved in the OpenClaw capability index.
skill.summary: Routes Codex to 869 OpenClaw medical and biomedical capabilities covering clinical, genomics, drug discovery, bioinformatics, and regulatory workflows.
skill.author: Yuan1z0825

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 234 potential issues across 4 files (3,657 lines), but evaluation confirms nearly all are false positives. The 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' findings (e.g., MD5/SHA) are matching the medical acronyms 'MD' (Medical Doctor) and 'AMP' (ACMG/AMP, AMP/ASCO/CAP) used throughout the capability index. The 'Windows SAM database' findings are matching the bioinformatics file format 'SAM/BAM' (Sequence Alignment Map). 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings are matching legitimate markdown inline code like `bcftools consensus`. The 'Hidden file in home directory' findings are matching documented installation paths like `~/.openclaw/skills/`. No actual code execution, exfiltration, or credential access patterns were found. The skill is a routing/reference document with safety boundaries; no executable scripts exist.

medium_findings.title: Reference to Public URLs (Potential Network Use)
medium_findings.description: The capability index and source documentation reference public URLs such as GitHub repositories, PubMed, ClinicalTrials.gov, ChEMBL, and other scientific databases. These are legitimate external resources for medical research workflows. When the skill is invoked via Codex/Claude, the underlying tools may perform HTTP requests to these endpoints to fetch data. No evidence of data exfiltration to attacker-controlled endpoints.

low_findings.title: Documentation References to User Home Directory Paths
low_findings.description: Documentation describes installation paths under the user home directory (e.g., `~/.openclaw/skills/`, `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json`). These are legitimate installation instructions, not code that accesses hidden files. The skill itself does not read or write hidden files at runtime.

low_findings.title: Markdown Inline Code with Backticks
low_findings.description: Markdown content uses backticks for inline code formatting (e.g., `bcftools consensus`, `rg -n`). These are documentation formatting, not Ruby/shell command execution. No command injection risk.

low_findings.title: Medical Acronyms Triggering Hash Algorithm Patterns
low_findings.description: Capability index uses medical acronyms 'MD' (Medical Doctor), 'AMP' (ACMG/AMP, AMP/ASCO/CAP), and 'SHA' (likely false match) that the static scanner misidentified as weak cryptographic algorithms (MD5, SHA-1). No actual cryptographic code is present.

low_findings.title: Bioinformatics File Format Acronym 'SAM' Misidentified
low_findings.description: The bioinformatics file format SAM/BAM (Sequence Alignment Map / Binary Alignment Map) is mentioned in capability descriptions. The static scanner flagged 'SAM' as Windows SAM database. No Windows credential store access is intended or possible.

content.user_title: Route medical and biomedical tasks with OpenClaw
content.value_statement: Medical and biomedical tasks require navigating hundreds of specialized capabilities. This skill routes Codex to the right OpenClaw capability for clinical, genomics, drug discovery, bioinformatics, and regulatory workflows. It enforces safety boundaries so outputs support, never replace, qualified clinicians.
actual_capabilities: Routes Codex to 869 OpenClaw medical capabilities across 8 categories: general, clinical, scientific databases, bioinformatics, omics, ClawBio pipelines, BioOS extensions, and data science.
Searches capability index by skill slug, category, or task description to identify the right tool for a request.
Classifies requests by risk and domain (patient care, clinical research, literature review, drug/variant interpretation, bioinformatics, regulatory).
Provides output standards by task: plain-language clinical summaries, evidence-graded literature reviews, drug research with mechanism and monitoring, and ACMG-compliant variant interpretation.
Enforces safety boundaries: outputs are for education, research, drafting, and decision support only, never definitive diagnosis or prescription.
Recommends primary sources (PubMed, ClinicalTrials.gov, FDA/EMA labels, ClinVar, gnomAD, COSMIC, OMIM, Orphanet, HPO) over fabricated data.
limitations: Does not include the actual OpenClaw or NanoClaw runtime; assumes Codex-native tools, local files, Python/R, and web access.
Some capabilities require credentials, MCP endpoints, or local databases not available in every Codex session; skill will state the limitation rather than fabricate results.
Not a substitute for qualified clinicians, pharmacists, genetic counselors, or regulatory professionals.
Capability index is a routing reference, not an executable skill set; downstream execution depends on the Codex environment.

use_cases.title: Clinical Literature Review for a Clinician
use_cases.description: A clinician needs an evidence summary on a drug interaction for a complex patient. The skill routes to PubMed search and drug-drug interaction analysis, returns graded evidence with source dates and citations.

use_cases.title: Genomic Variant Interpretation for a Researcher
use_cases.description: A genomics researcher has a VCF file and needs ACMG-classified variant interpretation. The skill routes to variant analysis tools, integrates ClinVar and gnomAD annotations, and produces a decision-support draft for qualified genetics review.

use_cases.title: Drug Discovery Pipeline for a Biotech Analyst
use_cases.description: A biotech analyst needs target intelligence, ADMET profile, and repurposing candidates for a compound. The skill routes to drug research, chemical safety, and network pharmacology capabilities, returning structured reports with primary-source citations.
use_cases.target_user: Drug discovery and translational science teams

prompt_templates.title: Beginner: Find the right capability
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a task involving [clinical topic / dataset / drug name / gene]. Search the OpenClaw capability index and tell me which 2-3 capabilities best match, with a one-line summary of each.

prompt_templates.title: Intermediate: Classify and route a research request
prompt_templates.prompt: Classify this request by risk and domain, then route it to the best OpenClaw capability: [paste your biomedical or clinical request]. State the chosen capability, why it fits, and what primary sources you will use.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced: Multi-capability drug interaction analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the drug-drug interaction and pharmacovigilance capabilities, analyze the interaction between [Drug A] and [Drug B] for a patient on [Drug C]. Include CYP450 mechanism, severity, monitoring, alternatives, and escalation criteria. Cite the label and guideline sources with retrieval dates.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced: ACMG variant interpretation workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Walk me through ACMG/AMP variant interpretation for [gene] [variant, e.g., BRCA1 c.5266dupC]. Route to variant interpretation and cancer variant interpretation capabilities, integrate ClinVar/gnomAD/COSMIC, and produce a draft classification for qualified genetics review. Mark all outputs as decision support, not diagnosis.

output_examples.input: User asks: 'I need a literature review on SGLT2 inhibitors in heart failure with preserved ejection fraction.'
output_examples.output: The skill classifies the request as literature review (clinical research), routes to PubMed search and literature-review capabilities, and returns: search strategy (PubMed, date range, MeSH terms), inclusion criteria, evidence table with study type and PMID, summary distinguishing guidelines from randomized trials, and a plain-language clinical bottom line with red flags and professional-review recommendation.

output_examples.input: User asks: 'Interpret BRCA1 c.5266dupC for a hereditary cancer risk assessment.'
output_examples.output: The skill routes to variant interpretation capability, retrieves ClinVar and gnomAD population frequency, applies ACMG criteria (PVS1, PS3, PM2), produces a draft pathogenic classification with evidence citations, and explicitly marks the output as decision support requiring qualified genetics review. It does not provide a clinical recommendation.

best_practices: Always state the chosen capability and primary sources before producing a draft; transparency beats silent routing.
De-identify patient information (names, dates, identifiers) before using clinical examples; keep analyses local unless the user asks otherwise.
Mark every clinical or regulatory output as a draft requiring qualified professional review; never present as definitive diagnosis or clearance.
Cite retrieval dates for time-sensitive facts (FDA labels, trial status, guidelines) so users know how current the evidence is.
anti_patterns: Do not present the skill's output as a definitive diagnosis, prescription, treatment order, or substitute for a qualified clinician.
Do not fabricate patient data, lab values, guideline recommendations, regulatory status, trial availability, or database results when the source is unavailable.
Do not skip the safety boundary or crisis routing: emergencies, self-harm risk, and severe acute symptoms must trigger immediate emergency-services guidance.

faq.question: What is OpenClaw Medical Skills?
faq.answer: It is a community library of 869 medical and biomedical AI capabilities covering clinical research, genomics, drug discovery, bioinformatics, and regulatory workflows. This skill adapts the library for Codex and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does this skill execute medical tasks directly?
faq.answer: No. It is a routing and execution guide. It identifies the right OpenClaw capability, classifies the task, and translates it into Codex-native steps using local files, Python, web access, and installed tools.

faq.question: Can I use this for actual patient diagnosis?
faq.answer: No. Outputs are for education, research, drafting, triage support, and decision support only. They must be reviewed by a qualified clinician before any clinical action.

faq.question: Where does the skill get its medical data?
faq.answer: It routes to primary sources such as PubMed, ClinicalTrials.gov, FDA/EMA labels, ClinVar, gnomAD, COSMIC, OMIM, Orphanet, HPO, ChEMBL, DrugBank, and similar databases. It prefers primary sources and cites retrieval dates.

faq.question: What happens if a required database or MCP endpoint is unavailable?
faq.answer: The skill states the limitation explicitly, then uses the best available primary source or asks the user for credentials or local files only when needed. It never fabricates results.

faq.question: Which AI tools does this skill support?
faq.answer: It works with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code. The skill ships an openai.yaml agent definition and a SKILL.md router that adapts to whichever tool is invoking it.
